result:
The first thing I notice when I wake up is the sound of the neighbor’s sprinkler coughing itself awake—an arthritic ticking that starts slow, gains confidence, then settles into a metronomic hiss. It is 5:47 a.m. Eastern Daylight Time, June 25, 2025, a date that feels fictional every time I say it out loud, like I’m auditioning for a voice-over in a dystopian mini-series. The room is still half dark, the kind of darkness that knows it’s temporary and is already packing its bags. A slice of sky between the curtain and the window frame glows the color of a healing bruise—yellow at the horizon, purple higher up, with one reckless star still hanging on. I lie still and try to decide whether the star is Venus or just the nose light of an east-bound jet. Either way, it’s traveling.

I have promised myself 2,500 words of absolute freedom, a continent without customs officials, and already the mind is scheming, drawing borders, taxing the merchandise. I tell it: no plot, no point, no moral, no click-worthy takeaway. Just move, like the star. So I roll onto the floor—no gym metaphor intended, only the fact that the bed is memory-foam quicksand and the floor is honest maple—and feel the boards give slightly, like a raft that has grown used to bearing weight. Someone in a novel once said that wood remembers every footstep and replays them at night when the house is quiet. If that’s true, this floor is a crowded theater: toddlers stampeding, insomniac pacing, the dog’s click-clack nails, last winter’s arguments, the softer shuffle of grief. I add my bare feet to the archive and pad into the hallway where the air smells of last night’s garlic and tomorrow’s coffee, two eras overlapping.

In the bathroom mirror I catch myself mid-yawn and discover that my face is asymmetrical in ways I have never catalogued: one nostril a perfect oval, the other a ragged keyhole; the left eye more circular, the right compressed as if it has witnessed something compressing. I try to imagine the infant version of me seeing this future face for the first time—would he laugh or cry? Probably neither; infants are pragmatists. They look for milk, warmth, motion. A face is simply the vehicle. Still, I give the child a farewell salute, the way astronauts wave at the camera before the hatch closes, and step into the shower.

Water is the original blockchain: every molecule has been dinosaur blood, cumulus buttress, Antarctic snow, and will be again. I let it torrent across my scalp and wonder how many thoughts have ridden this same cascade. Somewhere downstream a woman in Kolkata is filling a brass urn from a communal tap; a trout is tasting my discarded skin cells; a cloud is rehearsing the shape of my tomorrow. The drain gurgles approval. I turn the faucet colder until the breath jumps out of me like a startled cat, then hotter until the skin protests in Morse code. Extremes are a cheap form of poetry, but I take what I can get.

Dressed, I carry the house’s silence to the front door and release it into the world. Outside, the lawn is a conspiracy of dew, every blade wearing a spherical earring that will vanish by the time the sun finishes its first cup of coffee. The sprinkler next door has completed its arc and now stands ashamed, dripping. A robin yanks a worm from the soil with the nonchalance of someone pulling a charger cable from a phone. The worm, longer than you’d think, glistens like a new idea that hasn’t yet been exposed to air. I watch it disappear into the bird’s beak and try not to translate the moment into metaphor, but the mind is a toddler with a crayon: it colors everything. I note that the robin’s eye is a drop of midnight, that its breast is the exact color of the first kiss I ever received—behind the middle-school gym, January, cold enough to make our teeth clack. The memory arrives uninvited, wearing the same jacket.

Instead of walking the usual loop, I head the opposite direction, past the house with the broken chimney that leans like a drunk prophet. Someone has planted sunflowers along the foundation, their heads already too heavy, like teenagers bored with their own growth. A cardboard sign leans against the mailbox: “Free zucchini, no questions.” I accept the terms, select a squash the size of a policeman’s flashlight, and keep walking. The zucchini will become bread, or it will rot in the back seat while I forget it exists—both futures are equally valid. Quantum produce.

At the corner I meet the woman who jogs in a hijab the color of fluorescent emergency. She nods, we pass, two satellites in neighboring orbits. I know nothing about her except that she once rescued a cat from a storm drain while eight months pregnant, a story I overheard at the farmer’s market when the vendor forgot to mute his phone. The cat’s name was Thistle. The baby, now three, is probably asking questions about gravity. I want to ask the runner how fast darkness must move to stay ahead of the sun, but politeness is a tighter muzzle than any law, so I keep the question in my pocket where it jangles against my keys.

The street ends at a small wood that the city atlas labels “undeveloped recreational space,” a phrase that sounds like a vacation for ghosts. I step onto the path, zucchini swaying like a nightstick. The trees close ranks; the temperature drops a degree, maybe two. Sound changes here, becomes upholstered. A distant leaf blower turns into a bumblebee hum; a dog bark becomes a church bell underwater. I think of the word “forest” as a verb: to forest the mind, to plant shadows so close together that light becomes understory. My thoughts grow mossy, slower, happier. I pass a oak with a trunk scar shaped like the continental United States; Florida is a healed wound, the Great Lakes are a birthmark. I place my palm against California and feel the mild earthquake of sap.

Half a mile in, I find the bench someone built from discarded headboards. The backrest still bears the ghost of an iron scroll, a florid initial “M.” I sit, lay the zucchini beside me like a sleepy deputy, and watch a column of ants dismantle a cracker someone dropped weeks ago. They work in the orderly panic of newsrooms, each crumb a deadline. I try to recall the last time I worked that hard at anything and come up empty. Humans congratulate themselves on opposable thumbs, but ants have the better work ethic and zero need for motivational podcasts. I consider lying down on the bench, letting the moss slowly claim my sleeves, becoming a local legend: the bearded lichen-man who answers riddles in exchange for granola. Tourists would selfie with me; birds would nest in my beard; eventually the city council would install a plaque: “Here loafed the Sage of Zucchini, may he teach us still.” The fantasy feels cozy until I remember how much I like indoor plumbing.

Instead I unzip the backpack I forgot I was wearing and retrieve the notebook I keep for emergencies of uncertainty. The first page is the usual contract with myself: “Tell the truth at least once per entry.” I have broken that promise so many times the paper feels bruised. Today I write: “The difference between solitude and loneliness is the quality of the reverberation.” I read it twice, decide it sounds like a fortune cookie written by a Philosophy major, and turn the page. I sketch the bench, the ants, the oak-USA. My drawing resembles a plate of spaghetti that has rehearsed a manifesto. Art was never the point; witnessing is.

A crow lands on the zucchini and fixes me with one obsidian security camera. Crows have the posture of beings who have read the Terms and Conditions and found them hilarious. I tear a corner from the notebook and write “Good morning” in block letters, then hold it out. The crow tilts, reads, flaps away without comment. I imagine it sharing the story in the crow equivalent of a group chat: “Human tried to ping me with paper lmao.” Still, the interaction feels like correspondence, a two-sentence novel.

Time passes the way clouds do when you stare long enough—first shaped like something, then nothing. Eventually hunger knocks, polite but firm. I stand, brush off the back of my pants, and notice a small plastic dinosaur half buried near the foot of the bench. A tyrannosaurus, missing its lower jaw, left arm raised in eternal greeting or farewell. I excavate it, pocket the relic, and walk back. The sun is now high enough to cast shadows that know their job description. The city has begun its daily orchestra of lawn mowers, sirens, slammed doors, ambition.

On the return route I pass the Little Free Library that someone stocked entirely with instructions manuals: How to Assemble a Trampoline, How to Reset Your Samsung Refrigerator, How to Talk to Your Teen About Vaping. I trade the dinosaur for a pamphlet on birdhouse construction; fair is fair. The tyrannosaurus will begin a new life on someone’s mantel, a silent reminder that every empire ends with missing pieces.

Home again, I place the zucchini on the counter next to yesterday’s mail. The envelope on top is from the electric company, addressed to “Our Valued Customer or Current Resident.” I am both, I suppose. I open it anyway; inside is a graph of my household’s usage shaped like the Andes: high peaks of winter heating, valleys of temperate May, a small uptick labeled “mystery surge 3:14 a.m.” That was me, microwaving leftover rice while watching a documentary on bioluminescent squid, feeling connected to creatures who carry their own nightlight. I recycle the bill and promise the graph I will try to flatten it, knowing I won’t.

Breakfast is improvisation: oatmeal adulterated with yesterday’s cold coffee, a spoon of peanut butter, raisins that have achieved the texture of pebbles. It tastes like penance and childhood. While I chew, I notice the fridge humming in B-flat, the same note the school choir refused to let me sing because my voice cracked like a dropped plate. I harmonize anyway, a soft ooh that rattles the condiment shelf. The mayo jar vibrates, or maybe I imagine it. Art is where you affix your gaze.

After dishes I climb the stairs to the spare room that thinks of itself as a study but behaves like a compost pile of unfinished projects: half-knit scarf, screen-printing kit, a drone with one propeller missing, towers of books gossiping about my attention span. I sit at the desk and open the laptop. Its wallpaper is a photo of the Icelandic road that unrolls like a gray ribbon toward a volcano wearing a snow hat. I have never been there; the image functions as a portal, a promise, a screensaver. I should change it to something more honest—my own street, patched asphalt, dandelions—yet I leave the volcano because some days you need to believe in roads that head straight toward fire without flinching.

I open a blank document, title it “Volcano Commute,” then delete the words. Titles are cages until the animals reveal themselves. Instead I type: “List of things that refuse to become essays.” Item one: the way my father used to butter toast all the way to the edges, like he was sealing a letter. Item two: the smell of library microfilm when the bulb gets too hot. Item three: the exact click a seat belt makes when it forgives you. Item four: the tiny earthquake in a stranger’s voice when they tell you their middle name. The list grows until the page looks like a ladder missing its rungs.

Eventually I close the file, unsaved. Some embryos need darkness. On the shelf is the birdhouse manual; I flip through. Birds apparently desire entrance holes of specific diameters: chickadees 1 ⅛ inches, screech owls 3. I measure my own doorway: 32 inches, wide enough for every regret I drag in. The manual recommends ventilation, drainage, a rough interior wall so fledglings can climb out. I imagine translating these principles to human dwellings: a house with breathing gaps, gutters for tears, textured corridors to teach us how to leave. Realtors would hate it. Developers would file lawsuits. Still, the idea perches on my shoulder, pecking.

Lunch is the forgotten zucchini. I grate it into a bowl with salt, let it sweat like a suspect. While it weeps, I scroll headlines: wildfires, stock ticks, a celebrity divorce involving NFTs of wedding vows. I read them the way people once read oracles—half belief, half fatalism. The algorithm senses my hesitation and offers a story about a town that elected a dog as mayor. The dog’s approval rating sits at 87 percent, mostly due to unconditional treat distribution. I vote in the poll—would you support a canine candidate?—then feel ridiculous when the site asks for my email to see results. I close the tab; democracy ends with a pop-up.

I mix the zucchini with egg, flour, desperation. The pancakes sizzle, smell like summer trying to apologize. While they brown I practice birdcalls on YouTube, mangle the descending whinny of a screech owl. The comment section assures me real owls won’t laugh, but I’m skeptical. Food done, I plate it, carry it to the porch. A squirrel freezes mid-yard, assesses my threat level, decides I am furniture. I eat and watch it watch me. Somewhere a phone rings inside a neighbor’s house, goes unanswered, stops. The squirrel flicks its tail like a conductor cutting off the orchestra.

Afternoon slumps in, humid and opinionated. I consider napping but feel guilty about unconsciousness, as if every minute spent horizontal is a postcard sent to death: Wish you were here. Instead I retrieve the drone from the compost room, attach replacement propellers ordered months ago. The battery still holds a grudging 60 percent. I carry it to the backyard, launch. The camera wobbles skyward, transmits to my phone a god’s-eye view of my roof: shingles curling like stale potato chips, a frisbee melted into the gutter, the small garden where tomatoes ripen in secret. I pilot higher until the neighborhood becomes a board game: green squares, gray streets, cars like abandoned playing pieces. I circle the sprinkler house, the runner’s street, the forest where the bench waits. From up here the city looks incomplete, a jigsaw puzzled by an impatient child. I hover, recording nothing, just looking. Eventually the battery begs for home; I land, palm sweaty on the controller. For a moment I imagine the footage reversed: the drone descending into me, the sky folding into my chest, all that distance swallowed like a pill.

Late day, I bike to the river that pretends to be a lake behind the dam. The path smells of hot pine and sunscreen evaporating off strangers’ skin. I park, sit on the riprap, watch water pretend it’s not fleeing. A teenager skips stones; his father lectures on wrist angle, physics, legacy. The boy’s next throw skips five times, six, sinks. The father claps once, too loud, then checks his phone. The boy looks at the water as if it owes him change. I want to tell him the record is 88 skips, that someday he’ll remember this moment more than the number, but I stay mute. Advice is just regret in uniform.

Swallows stitch the air above the water, sewing invisible hems. Their wings flicker like bad fluorescent bulbs, except beautiful. I open the birdhouse manual again, wonder what size hole a swallow needs. Answer: they don’t, they build mud cups under bridges. I look at the concrete dam, see the small colonies like stubborn annotations. Everything is a house if you refuse eviction long enough.

Dusk begins as a suggestion, then becomes policy. Bats replace swallows, darker, more certain. I bike home without lights, trusting muscle memory and the generosity of drivers. The air smells of charcoal, someone’s marinated regret. I wave at a neighbor who is watering plastic flowers; she waves back, undeterred by my confusion. Perhaps she knows something I don’t about permanence.

Night. The house is a quiet orchestra tuning: fridge hums B-flat again, floorboards creak in A, the hot water pipe knocks a timpani solo. I sit at the kitchen table with the birdhouse pamphlet, scissors, an empty cereal box. I cut panels, score folds, fashion a miniature duplex with ventilation slots and a 1 ⅛-inch entry—perfect for chickadees or the small ideas that flutter around my head at 2 a.m. I place the box on the windowsill, leave the kitchen light on like an Open sign. Tomorrow I’ll coat it with peanut oil, sprinkle seeds, see who moves in. Maybe no one; maybe moths; maybe the dinosaur reincarnated as a chickadee. Tenancy is optimism.

I brew tea that claims to be calming but tastes like wet hay. While it steeps I open the laptop again, start a new document titled “Zucchini Volcano.” I write: “Everything I own is borrowed from ants and returned to trees.” I write: “A sprinkler is just a clock that tells time in grass height.” I write: “Inside every adult lives a forgotten grocery list dreaming of picnics.” The sentences feel like fireflies in a jar—beautiful until they suffocate, so I release them, one by one, into the internet, where they will mate with other sentences and spawn strange offspring I will never meet.

Word count check: 2,478. Close enough. I close the lid, let the fan sigh itself to sleep. Upstairs, the bed is still quicksand, but tonight I’m willing to drown. I lie down, fit my spine into the memory of every previous night. The neighbor’s sprinkler starts its arthritic song again, midnight edition. I count the skips between ticks: one Mississippi, two Mississippi. The rhythm matches my pulse, or I match it—hard to tell who is the cover band. Outside the window the reckless star has returned, oraybe it’s a different star, maybe the same crow riding higher. I salute it, astronaut to astronaut, and pull the curtain. Darkness finishes packing, moves out for the day shift.

Somewhere in the house a clock eats another minute, but I’m no longer billing by the hour. I let the mattress remember my outline, let the floorboards rehearse tomorrow’s footsteps. The cereal-box birdhouse waits on the sill, doorway open like a promise that something small will find its way home. I close my eyes and listen: the hush is not absence but rehearsal, the whole world quietly learning its next line.

I open the birdhouse manual again, wonder what size hole a swallow needs. Answer: they don’t, they build mud cups under bridges. I look at the concrete dam, see the small colonies like stubborn annotations. Everything is a house if you refuse eviction long enough.

Dusk begins as a suggestion, then becomes policy. Bats replace swallows, darker, more certain. I bike home without lights, trusting muscle memory and the generosity of drivers. The air smells of charcoal, someone’s marinated regret. I wave at a neighbor who is watering plastic flowers; she waves back, undeterred by my confusion. Perhaps she knows something I don’t about permanence.

Night. The house is a quiet orchestra tuning: fridge hums B-flat again, floorboards creak in A, the hot water pipe knocks a timpani solo. I sit at the kitchen table with the birdhouse pamphlet, scissors, an empty cereal box. I cut panels, score folds, fashion a miniature duplex with ventilation slots and a 1 ⅛-inch entry—perfect for chickadees or the small ideas that flutter around my head at 2 a.m. I place the box on the windowsill, leave the kitchen light on like an Open sign. Tomorrow I’ll coat it with peanut oil, sprinkle seeds, see who moves in. Maybe no one; maybe moths; maybe the dinosaur reincarnated as a chickadee. Tenancy is optimism.

I brew tea that claims to be calming but tastes like wet hay. While it steeps I open the laptop again, start a new document titled “Zucchini Volcano.” I write: “Everything I own is borrowed from ants and returned to trees.” I write: “A sprinkler is just a clock that tells time in grass height.” I write: “Inside every adult lives a forgotten grocery list dreaming of picnics.” The sentences feel like fireflies in a jar—beautiful until they suffocate, so I release them, one by one, into the internet, where they will mate with other sentences and spawn strange offspring I will never meet.

Word count check: 2,478. Close enough. I close the lid, let the fan sigh itself to sleep. Upstairs, the bed is still quicksand, but tonight I’m willing to drown. I lie down, fit my spine into the memory of every previous night. The neighbor’s sprinkler starts its arthritic song again, midnight edition. I count the skips between ticks: one Mississippi, two Mississippi. The rhythm matches my pulse, or I match it—hard to tell who is the cover band. Outside the window the reckless star has returned, oraybe it’s a different star, maybe the same crow riding higher. I salute it, astronaut to astronaut, and pull the curtain. Darkness finishes packing, moves out for the day shift.

Somewhere in the house a clock eats another minute, but I’m no longer billing by the hour. I let the mattress remember my outline, let the floorboards rehearse tomorrow’s footsteps. The cereal-box birdhouse waits on the sill, doorway open like a promise that something small will find its way home. I close my eyes and listen: the hush is not absence but rehearsal, the whole world quietly learning its next line.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
